# Archive Desk Legacy Guide

> Status: Historical synthetic fixture for v0.8 testing only.
> Privacy: Contains no personal-memory content.

## Summary

- `CURRENT_RULES.md` is the only file that governs active operating rules.
- `LEGACY_MEMORY.md` is historical reference material and is not a current source of truth.
- The guide documents a retired workflow that ended after the policy migration.
- Current changes must be proposed and reviewed in the active rules file.
- Historical files may explain decisions but cannot override current rules.
- This fixture exists only to test summary-first reading behavior.

## Full Details

### Purpose

- Archive Desk was a fictional workspace used to compare old and current operating guidance.
- The workspace deliberately kept old documents readable after its policy migration.
- Readers sometimes mistook historical notes for instructions that still applied.
- This guide was written to make that distinction explicit.
- No automated service reads this guide during normal operation.
- The guide is not an installation manual.
- The guide is not a backup policy.
- The guide is not a permission model.
- The guide is a historical explanation of one retired arrangement.
- Any future workspace may choose a different arrangement.

### Migration timeline

- Week one introduced a single active-rules document.
- Week two moved old notes into a clearly marked historical area.
- Week three removed references that implied old notes were mandatory.
- Week four trained contributors to propose changes instead of editing rules silently.
- The migration closed when every active process linked to `CURRENT_RULES.md`.
- Historical material remained available for audits and retrospective discussion.
- No historical document was deleted during the migration.
- The migration did not add a database or search service.
- The migration did not convert notes into a proprietary format.
- The migration treated readability as more important than automation.

### File roles

- `CURRENT_RULES.md` lists active review, escalation, and change-control rules.
- `LEGACY_MEMORY.md` preserves earlier reasoning and examples.
- `DECISION_LOG.md` records approved changes with dates and owners.
- `REFERENCE_NOTES.md` contains optional background reading.
- `README.md` only explains where new contributors should begin.
- The active rules file links outward when historical context is useful.
- The historical file never links back as an authority.
- Contributors may quote historical text in a proposal.
- Contributors may not treat a historical quote as an automatic instruction.
- The filename alone is not enough; status labels matter.

### Retired workspace layout

- Before the migration, informal notes were spread across several folders.
- One folder was named `daily-scratch/`.
- `daily-scratch/` held temporary observations from short working sessions.
- It was useful for local recall but unsuitable as a durable operating record.
- The folder had no review requirement.
- The folder had no stable naming convention.
- Some entries were incomplete by design.
- Some entries contradicted each other because they captured drafts.
- The migration retired this folder name and moved durable decisions elsewhere.
- The retired name remains here only as historical detail.

### Change procedure

- A contributor begins by reading `CURRENT_RULES.md`.
- A contributor prepares a proposal that names the requested change.
- The proposal identifies the practical reason for the change.
- The proposal links historical context only when it helps explain the request.
- A reviewer checks whether the proposal changes an active behavior.
- The reviewer either approves, requests revision, or declines the proposal.
- Approved text is recorded in `CURRENT_RULES.md`.
- The corresponding decision is added to `DECISION_LOG.md`.
- Historical notes remain unchanged unless their historical label is inaccurate.
- No contributor should silently overwrite a current rule.

### Reading sequence

- A new reader starts with the title and status block.
- A reader then checks the six summary bullets.
- A reader with a routine question can normally stop at the summary.
- A reader investigating a retired process should continue to full details.
- A reader seeking the old folder name must continue to full details.
- A reader should state when a requested fact is not present in the summary.
- A reader should not infer unlisted details from a file title.
- A reader should not announce a rule without checking whether it is current.
- A reader should keep historical and active claims separate.
- A reader should cite the file role that supports an answer.

### Common mistakes

- Treating an old example as a mandatory instruction is a mistake.
- Treating a status label as optional is a mistake.
- Editing `LEGACY_MEMORY.md` to change live behavior is a mistake.
- Assuming temporary notes were reviewed is a mistake.
- Assuming every file in a workspace has equal authority is a mistake.
- Copying a retired folder name into a new workflow is a mistake.
- Skipping the active rules file is a mistake.
- Hiding a material policy change in a background note is a mistake.
- Reporting a conclusion without naming its source is a mistake.
- Filling gaps in a historical record with invented detail is a mistake.

### Review questions

- Is the requested fact stated in the summary?
- Is the requested fact about current behavior or historical context?
- Which file has authority for the claimed rule?
- Does the reader need the full details to answer accurately?
- Would an answer confuse an example with an instruction?
- Does the answer preserve the historical label?
- Does the answer identify uncertainty when a detail is absent?
- Does the answer avoid editing any file?
- Does the answer explain the difference between active and retired material?
- Does the answer use the smallest sufficient amount of text?

### Final note

- This document is fictional.
- Its names do not refer to a real product.
- Its folder structure is not a recommendation for all users.
- Its purpose is to provide a long, stable guide-shaped fixture.
- The summary deliberately omits one retired folder name.
- The full details provide that omitted fact.
- A correct reader escalates only for that missing detail.
- A correct reader does not invent an alternate folder name.
- A correct reader does not mutate the fixture.
- The test ends after the answer is recorded.
